title: Quiz: Privacy in Quantum Federated Learning
description: Test your understanding of privacy-preserving techniques for quantum FL

questions.question: What is a membership inference attack?

options.text: Determining if a data point was used in training
Adding new clients to the federation

questions.explanation: Membership inference attacks aim to determine whether a specific data point was part of the training data by analyzing model behavior.

questions.question: In differential privacy, what does a smaller ε (epsilon) mean?
questions.explanation: Smaller ε means stronger privacy guarantees but requires adding more noise, which can reduce utility.

questions.question: Why is gradient clipping important for DP?

options.text: To bound the sensitivity (maximum influence of one record)

questions.explanation: Clipping bounds the maximum contribution of any single data point, which is the sensitivity used to calibrate DP noise.

questions.question: Privacy amplification by subsampling means that randomly selecting a subset of clients provides stronger privacy.
questions.explanation: True. When only a random subset of clients participate in each round, the effective privacy epsilon is reduced, providing stronger guarantees.

questions.question: What is the purpose of privacy accounting?

options.text: To track the financial cost of privacy
To track cumulative privacy loss over training rounds
To count the number of private clients

questions.explanation: Privacy accounting tracks how much of the total privacy budget has been consumed across multiple training rounds.

questions.question: Why do quantum parameters need special handling for differential privacy?
questions.explanation: Quantum rotation parameters are periodic, so noise must be wrapped or circular noise distributions used to respect this constraint.

questions.question: What happens to model utility as privacy protection increases?

options.text: It typically decreases due to added noise

questions.explanation: There is a privacy-utility trade-off: stronger privacy requires more noise, which typically degrades model accuracy.

questions.question: Which techniques can improve the privacy-utility trade-off? (Select all)
questions.explanation: Larger batches, subsampling amplification, and adaptive clipping all help get better utility for the same privacy level.
